title: The Age of the Digital Butler
subtitle: AI agents are giving ordinary people the power of an organization. They cannot tell us what that power is for.
description: AI agents are giving ordinary people the power of an organization. They cannot tell us what that power is for.
body_html:
Recently, I stopped feeling as though I was using artificial intelligence and began feeling as though I was running a small company.

I had assembled a fleet of agents with different responsibilities. One could research. Another could examine an argument. Another could help organize production. A persistent agent could maintain the workflow, keep track of unfinished projects, and surface work that needed my attention. Instead of opening a chatbot and asking a question, I was beginning to direct a team.

The distinction is difficult to appreciate until you experience it.

Most people still encounter AI as a more articulate version of Google. They type a question into ChatGPT, receive an answer, and close the window. An agent works differently. You give it an objective. It can break that objective into steps, use tools, work across files, coordinate with other systems, and return when it needs judgment.

The chatbot answers. The agent acts.

This does not make the agent a person. It does change the place the machine occupies in our lives.

From Tool to Organization

The arrival of Kimi K3 made this shift harder to dismiss. Moonshot AI, a Chinese company, describes it as a model reported at 2.8 trillion parameters, built for long-range coding, knowledge work, vision, and reasoning, with an open-weight release announced. Open-weight does not mean free to operate or completely transparent. It means the trained parameters can be made available for others to run and adapt rather than remaining entirely behind one company’s walls.

Kimi is significant partly because it is another reminder that advanced intelligence will not belong to one laboratory, one nation, or one subscription window. Capabilities that recently seemed confined to the richest American companies are spreading into a larger ecosystem. More organizations will be able to place increasingly capable intelligence inside their own products, workflows, robots, and agents.

The change is not only that the models are getting better, the workflow is changing around them.

In Humanity in the Age of AI, I describe four developments arriving together: intelligence, autonomy, interface, and embodiment. Intelligence gives the system the capacity to interpret and plan. Autonomy lets it continue acting toward a delegated objective. Interface makes it present. Embodiment gives it hands.

Together, these form a new digital layer between human intention and the world.

When My Agent Meets Yours

Imagine needing to arrange a meeting two years from now. Your agent knows your calendar, your travel preferences, the work you are trying to protect, and which relationships matter most. It contacts another person’s agent. The two systems compare schedules, negotiate a location, reserve a table, and place the meeting on both calendars.

That is a genuine gift, and I would surrender the administrative friction gladly.

But the same structure will spread. Agents will read and sort correspondence before we see it. They will draft replies in our voices. They will negotiate purchases, filter requests, propose relationships, and decide which interruptions deserve to reach us.

Before long, my digital representative may encounter yours before I encounter you.

That is more than saved time. It changes the structure of encounter itself. A mediating layer begins interpreting each person to the other, deciding what deserves attention before either human being is present. It may represent us accurately. It may even help us reach people we otherwise would have missed. But it will also make it easier to live inside a carefully managed world in which very little reaches us without first being filtered.

Something extraordinary is being offered to ordinary people. For most of history, only rulers, industrialists, and large institutions could command this degree of coordinated assistance. They had secretaries, researchers, advisers, couriers, and household staff. Agentic systems begin to place some of that capacity in the hands of an individual.

It feels a little like acquiring a digital butler.

That abundance is fundamentally good. Administrative work is not sacred merely because it is tedious. A tool that helps a small business owner organize a company, a parent recover time, or a creator bring worthy work into the world can serve human flourishing.

But abundance does not form the person receiving it.

The Time We Never Get Back

Every new layer of efficiency arrives with the same promise: once this burden is removed, we will finally have time for what matters. Then the calendar clears, and we fill it again. Capacity expands, expectations rise, and yesterday’s extraordinary output becomes tomorrow’s minimum.

We may become five times more productive without becoming any less busy.

That possibility feels especially real to me now. Having an agent team does not merely allow me to finish the work I was already doing. It allows me to imagine more work: another article, another video, another project. AI can shift us from passive consumption toward creation, which is often healthier. But creation can become another way of fleeing silence.

Busyness can make us feel significant. It assures us that we are needed, moving, producing, becoming. An agent does not heal that hunger. It gives the hunger more reach.

This is why efficiency alone cannot give us our lives back. It can return hours to the calendar. It cannot teach us how to receive them.

The Cost of Removing Friction

I notice this in a much smaller place: the grocery store.

When I am tired, the self-checkout line is appealing. I do not have to speak. I do not have to wait for the slight unpredictability of another person. I can scan my items and leave.

There is nothing morally wrong with self-checkout. But convenience repeatedly offers us the route that asks the least of us relationally. Delivery lets us avoid the store. A generated response lets us avoid finding the words. Each decision is minor. Together they can produce a life with remarkably little unplanned human contact.

Quality matters more than quantity in relationships. Yet brief encounters with cashiers, neighbors, and people whose lives do not naturally overlap with ours keep us in a shared world. They remind us that other people are not obstacles in an optimized day.

In a 2026 message about artificial intelligence and communication, Pope Leo XIV warned that the central challenge is “not technological, but anthropological.” Systems that imitate relationship can become a world of mirrors, shielding us from the difference and demands of another person (Message for the 60th World Day of Social Communications, January 24, 2026).

The danger is not that a machine will become human.

It is that humans will prefer whatever requires less of them than another human being does.

Learning to Dismiss Them

Christianity does not treat embodiment as an unfortunate limitation. The Word became flesh (John 1:14). Christ did not save humanity by transmitting better information from a safe distance. He entered a body, a family, a place, and history. He ate with people. He touched the sick. He allowed himself to be interrupted.

An incarnational life will become more deliberate as the digital layer thickens.

We will need to let agents arrange the meeting and then actually attend it. We will need to let them clear the schedule and then resist filling every opening. We will need to decide that prayer is not another task to optimize, summarize, or complete, but a place where our activity finally yields to presence before God.

Even prayer can become another form of doing. I read, speak, evaluate, ask, and move on. Stillness exposes the part of me that does not know how to exist without producing something. That discomfort will not disappear when our assistants become more capable. It may become easier to avoid.

The spiritual discipline of the agent economy will therefore be enoughness.

Enough work. Enough output. Enough improvement. Enough opportunities pursued simply because they became possible.

The real measure of abundance will not be how much our agents allow us to produce. It will be whether their service creates room for worship, friendship, attention, and the kind of face-to-face encounter in which neither person can be reduced to a pattern.

Soon, many of us may possess capacities once reserved for kings. We may each have researchers, organizers, editors, and digital servants ready at any hour.

The rarest form of wealth will be knowing when to dismiss them.

Subscribe now

Sources

  • Moonshot AI, “Kimi K3,” July 2026: https://www.moonshot.cn/

  • Moonshot AI model repository: https://huggingface.co/moonshotai

  • Pope Leo XIV, Message for the 60th World Day of Social Communications, January 24, 2026: https://press.vatican.va/content/salastampa/it/bollettino/pubblico/2026/01/24/0064.pdf

If this reframe reached something in you, pass it to someone who already feels five times more productive and no less busy.

The Inner Exodus stays free on purpose. If you want to keep it that way for the next person who needs it, you can become a patron below.
